coredata.dc:title: Copper nanoclusters/polydopamine nanospheres based fluorescence aptasensor for protein kinase activity determination
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Mengke
Wang, Shun
Su, Dandan
Su, Xingguang
coredata.dc:description: A fluorescence aptasensor was constructed for protein kinase (PKA) activity detection by utilizing copper nanoclusters (CuNCs) and polydopamine nanospheres (PDANS). Through the π-π stacking interactions between adenosine triphosphate (ATP) aptamer and PDANS, the ATP aptamer modified CuNCs (apt-CuNCs) were absorbed onto PDANS surface, thus the fluorescence of apt-CuNCs were quenched through fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) from apt-CuNCs to PDANS. In the presence of ATP, ATP specifically bound to aptamer, causing the dissociation of apt-CuNCs from PDANS surface and restoring the fluorescence of apt-CuNCs. However, PKA translated ATP into adenosine diphosphate (ADP), and ADP had no competence to combine with ATP aptamer, thus, apt-CuNCs were released and absorbed onto the PDANS surface to cause the fluorescence quenching of apt-CuNCs again. Therefore, PKA activity was conveniently detected via the fluorescence signal change. Under the optimal conditions, PKA activity was detected in the range of 0.05–4.5 U mL−1 with a detection limit of 0.021 U mL−1. Furthermore, the feasibility of the aptasensor for kinase inhibitor screening was explored via assessment of kinase inhibitor H-89 as one model. This aptasensor was also performed for PKA activity determination in HepG2 cell lysates with satisfactory results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescence
Polydopamine nanospheres
Protein kinase activity
Kinase inhibitor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of PKA activity and its inhibitor
Detection of PKA in HepG2 cell lysates
Detection of the PKA activity and its inhibitor
PKA determination in HepG2 cell lysates

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of CuNCs. Inset showed high magnification TEM (top) and the size distribution of CuNCs (bottom). (b) Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of CuNCs. Inset showed the photographs of CuNCs solution taken under the visible light (left) and 365 nm UV light (right). (c) UV-vis absorption spectrum of PDANS. (d) FI-IR spectra of dopamine and PDANS.
Fluorescence spectra of apt-CuNCs, apt-CuNCs/PKA, apt-CuNCs/ATP, apt-CuNCs/PKA/ATP, apt-CuNCs/PDANS, apt-CuNCs/PKA/PDANS, apt-CuNCs/ATP/PDANS, and apt-CuNCs/PKA/ATP/PDANS system, respectively.

(a) Effect of PDANS concentration on the fluorescence spectra of apt-CuNCs/PDANS system. Inset: the relationship between FL intensity ratio I/I0 and the concentration of PDANS. The concentration of PDANS from top to bottom was 0, 0.5, 1.5, 5, 10, 16, 20, 27, 32 and 40 μg mL−1. I0 and I were the fluorescence intensity of apt-CuNCs/PDANS system in the absence and presence of PDANS, respectively. Conditions: pH 7.4. (b) Effect of ATP concentration on the FL intensity of apt-CuNCs/PDANS system, from left to right: 0, 20, 40, 60, 80, 100, 120 and 140 μM, respectively. Conditions: 32 μg mL−1 PDANS, pH 7.4. (c) Effect of reaction time between PDANS and apt-CuNC on the FL intensity of apt-CuNCs/PDANS system, from left to right: 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 6 and 8 min, respectively. Conditions: 32 μg mL−1 PDANS, pH 7.4. (d) Effect of reaction time between ATP and aptamer of apt-CuNC on the FL intensity of apt-CuNCs/ATP/PDANS system, from left to right: 0, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30 and 40 min, respectively. Conditions: 32 μg mL−1 PDANS, 100 μM ATP, pH 7.4. The error bars were obtained from three parallel test results.

Fluorescence spectra of apt-CuNCs/ATP/PDANS system in the presence of PKA with different concentrations. Insert was the linearity of the fluorescence intensity ratio I/I0 versus the PKA concentration. I0 and I were the fluorescence intensity of apt-CuNCs/ATP/PDANS system in the absence and presence of PKA, respectively. Conditions: 100 μM ATP, 32 μg mL−1 PDANS, pH 7.4. The error bars were obtained from three parallel test results.

(a) The selectivity of the aptasensor for PKA activity detection. PKA concentration was 4.5 U mL−1, and trypsin, urease, GOx, tyrosinase and HRP concentrations were 9.0 U mL−1. I0 and I were the fluorescence intensity of apt-CuNCs/ATP/PDANS system in the absence and presence of enzyme, respectively. (b) The relationship between inhibition efficiency (IE) and H-89 concentration, from left to right: 0, 0.02, 0.05, 0.07, 0.1, 0.2, 0.3, 0.4, 0.6, 0.8, 1 and 1.3 μM, respectively. Conditions: 100 μM ATP, 32 μg mL−1 PDANS, 4.5 U mL−1 PKA, pH 7.4. The error bars were obtained from three parallel test results.
Schematic illustration of the fluorescent aptasensor for PKA activity detection.
PKA activity detection in HepG2 cell lysates.

head.ce:title: Copper nanoclusters/polydopamine nanospheres based fluorescence aptasensor for protein kinase activity determination

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Analytical Chemistry, College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun 130012, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Inorganic Synthesis and Preparative Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun 130012, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Inorganic Synthesis and Preparative Chemistry

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A fluorescence aptasensor was constructed for protein kinase (PKA) activity detection by utilizing copper nanoclusters (CuNCs) and polydopamine nanospheres (PDANS). Through the π-π stacking interactions between adenosine triphosphate (ATP) aptamer and PDANS, the ATP aptamer modified CuNCs (apt-CuNCs) were absorbed onto PDANS surface, thus the fluorescence of apt-CuNCs were quenched through fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) from apt-CuNCs to PDANS. In the presence of ATP, ATP specifically bound to aptamer, causing the dissociation of apt-CuNCs from PDANS surface and restoring the fluorescence of apt-CuNCs. However, PKA translated ATP into adenosine diphosphate (ADP), and ADP had no competence to combine with ATP aptamer, thus, apt-CuNCs were released and absorbed onto the PDANS surface to cause the fluorescence quenching of apt-CuNCs again. Therefore, PKA activity was conveniently detected via the fluorescence signal change. Under the optimal conditions, PKA activity was detected in the range of 0.05–4.5 U mL−1 with a detection limit of 0.021 U mL−1. Furthermore, the feasibility of the aptasensor for kinase inhibitor screening was explored via assessment of kinase inhibitor H-89 as one model. This aptasensor was also performed for PKA activity determination in HepG2 cell lysates with satisfactory results.

ce:section.ce:para: Protein kinases catalyze the phosphorylation of specific peptides or protein substrates by means of transferring a phosphate group from adenosine triphosphate (ATP), meanwhile, ATP is translated into adenosine diphosphate (ADP) by protein kinases [1, 2]. Protein phosphorylation based on protein kinases-catalyzed is a pivotal posttranslational modification and plays a key part in regulating numerous cellular biological processes including metabolism, cell cycle progression and differentiation, cellular signal communications, gene expression and apoptosis [3,4]. Also, the dysfunction of protein kinases has been closely associated with many complex diseases, such as cancer [5], HIV [6] and Alzheimer's disease [7]. Moreover, with human genome sequence completed, 518 putative protein kinases, which were encoded within the genome, have been identified, constituting around 1.7% of all human genes [8]. Specially to deserve to be mentioned, protein kinases have become significant targets for disease detection and the development of cancer drugs [9]. As a result, the identification and detection of protein kinases activity and inhibitors is of extreme significance in terms of clinical diagnosis and drug discovery.

Many strategies have been proposed for protein kinase activity detection, such as colorimetric method based on gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) aggregation behavior [10], fluorescence quenching of peptide biomineralized gold nanoclusters triggered by Zr4+ ion coordination based fluorescent assay [11], electrochemical biosensor based on the recognition utility of phos-tag for kinase-induced phosphopeptides and enzymatic signal amplification [12] and so on. Among these methods, fluorescent method draws more attention owing to its high sensitivity, simple operation and high-throughput capability. For example, Huang's group designed a sensitive fluorescent platform for monitoring protein kinase activity based on positively charged AuNPs and fluorescein isothiocyanate (FITC) labeled peptide [13]. Zhou and his coworkers proposed a novel fluorometric platform for protein kinase activity detection based on graphene oxide/FITC-labeled peptide probe nanocomplexs and phosphorylation-induced suppression of carboxypeptidase Y (CPY) cleavage [14]. Nevertheless, most of these fluorescent sensing strategies need tedious experiment design, extra enzymatic hydrolysis procedures or special dyes labels. Thus, it still remains an enormous challenge to develop cost-effective, simple and label-free assays for protein kinase activity detection.

Fluorescent metal nanoclusters (MNCs), composed of two or more metal atoms, have witnessed tremendous growth as a promising substitute for organic fluorescent dyes and inorganic semiconductor quantum dots for various biomedical applications owing to their large Stokes shift, strong photoluminescence, good biocompatibility, photostability, and desirable photophysical properties [15,16]. Among these MNCs, copper is abundant and relatively cheap, so the cost for CuNCs preparation is obviously lower compared with AgNCs and AuNCs [17]. Recently, various stabilizers have been proposed for the preparation of fluorescent CuNCs, such as proteins [18], nucleotides [19] and thiols [20]. Also, CuNCs have been highly applied as fluorescence probes for sensing enzyme activities [21], biomolecules [22] and cancer cell imaging [23] due to their low toxicity, good water solubility and excellent stability.

Fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) based sensors, which depend on the construction of various energy donor/acceptor pairs, have attracted an explosion of attention in the field of environmental and biological sensing [24–26]. To date, different nanomaterial-based fluorescent biosensors have been exploited via applying a series of quenchers, such as AuNPs [27], carbon nanotubes (CNTs) [28], graphene oxide (GO) [29], and metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) [30]. Nevertheless, the lacking water dispersibility, poor biocompatibility, high cytotoxicity or complicated preparation methods of these nanomaterials somewhat restrict their much wider applications in fluorescent biosensing. Therefore, the development of new nanoquenchers is of extreme importance to overcome these shortcomings. Recently, based on possessing excellent properties such as good biodegradability, biocompatibility, low cytotoxicity, simple synthesis processes, and tunable diameters, polydopamine nanospheres (PDANS) have emerged as one new kind of fascinating nanoquencher for biological sensing [31,32].

Here CuNCs/PDANS based fluorescent aptasensor was constructed for the simple, economical and sensitive detection of protein kinase (PKA) activity. The detection strategy was demonstrated in Scheme 1 . The ATP aptamer modified CuNCs (apt-CuNCs) were obtained through a traditional EDC/NHS condensation reaction between the carboxy group of CuNCs and amino group of aptamer. The adsorption of PDANS to ATP aptamer via π-π stacking interactions guaranteed the close proximity of apt-CuNCs to PDANS surface, resulting in the fluorescence quenching of apt-CuNCs via FRET from apt-CuNCs to PDANS. After ATP addition, the binding of aptamer with ATP disturbed the affinity between aptamer and PDANS, which caused the dissociation of the apt-CuNCs from PDANS surface and brought the fluorescence recovery. With PKA treatment, ATP was translated into ADP, leading to the release of aptamer, thus apt-CuNCs were absorbed onto PDANS surface to cause the fluorescence quenching of apt-CuNCs again. Therefore, PKA activity was conveniently detected via the fluorescence signal change. To our knowledge, the integration of nanoclusters with PDANS for developing FRET-based sensing strategies for protein kinase activity detection has not been explored yet.

The oligonucleotide (5′-NH2-(CH2)6-ACCTGGGGGAGTATTGCGGAGGAAGGT-3′) was synthesized by Shanghai Sangon Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). PKA, adenosine triphosphate (ATP) disodium salt hydrate, histidine, glucose oxidase (GOx), horseradish peroxidase (HRP), trypsin, tyrosinase, urease, and N-[2-(p-Bromocinnamylamino)ethyl]-5-isoquinolinesulfonamide dihydrochloride (H-89) were bought from Sigma-Aldrich Trading Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Dopamine hydrochloride, 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)carbodiimide (EDC), N-hydroxylsuccinimide sodium salt (NHS), copper (II) nitrate trihydrate (Cu(NO3)2·3H2O), ascorbic acid and tris(hydroxymethyl)aminomethane (Tris) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All other chemicals of analytical reagent grade were purchased from Sinopharm Group Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China) and used without further purification. Ultrapure water applied throughout the whole experiment had a resistivity higher than 18 MΩ·cm−1.

Fluorescence spectra were obtained by an F-2700 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). Ultraviolet-visible (UV-vis) absorption spectra were recorded using a Shimadzu UV-1700 UV-vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were performed on a Nicolet 6700 FT-IR spectrometer with KBr pellets (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, MA). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were captured by using a Hitachi H-800 electron microscope at an acceleration voltage of 200 kV with a CCD camera (Hitachi, Japan). Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were obtained on a JSM-6700F electron microscope (Tokyo, Japan). All pH measurements were performed on a PHS-3C pH meter (Hangzhou, China).

The fluorescent CuNCs were synthesized according to a modified method reported previously [33]. In brief, 360 μL Cu(NO3)2 solution (10 mM) was added into 8.0 mL histidine solution (100 mM). Followed by this, 400 μL ascorbic acid solution (100 mM) was added with stirring for 2 min. After that, the mixture was left to stand at room temperature without stirring for 24 h. The as-synthesized CuNCs stabilized by histidine were dialyzed using a dialysis tube (MWCO 1000 Da) to remove the impurities. Finally, the obtained CuNCs solution was filtered by passing through 0.22 μm filters to remove larger precipitation. The purified CuNCs solution was stored at 4 °C prior before further use.

For the preparation of apt-CuNCs, 1 mL as-prepared CuNCs solution was mixed with 1 mg EDC and 0.5 mg NHS for 1 h with vigorous stirring to activate the carboxyl group of CuNCs. Subsequently, 200 μL amino groups modified ATP aptamer (6 μM) was added into the mixture solution with continuous stirring overnight. The apt-CuNCs were obtained by means of the carboxyl group of CuNCs reacting with the amino group of aptamer via an amide coupling reaction to lead to the covalent attachment of aptamer to CuNCs. The fluorescence quantum yield of apt-CuNCs was 1.24% by using quinine sulfate as the reference.

PDANS were prepared according to the previous report with minor modification [34]. Briefly, 100 mg dopamine hydrochloride was added into a mixture of 100 mL Tris-buffer (10 mM) and 40 mL isopropyl alcohol with stirring. After continuous stirring for 48 h in the dark, the obtained solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm (centrifugal force 6820 g) for 10 min and washed with ultrapure water several times until the supernatant as colorless as water. At last, the precipitate was dried and redispersed in water to obtain 0.2 mg mL−1 PDANS stock solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of PKA activity and its inhibitor
ce:section.ce:para: The measurement of PKA activity was as follows. In brief, 80 μL ATP (500 μM) and 10 μL different concentrations of PKA were mixed with 110 μL Tris-HCl buffer (20 mM, pH = 7.4, containing 10 mM MgCl2 and 50 mM KCl). After reacted for 1 h at 37 °C, 100 μL apt-CuNCs and 36 μL Tris-HCl buffer were added and the resulting mixture was incubated for 20 min at ambient temperature. Then, 64 μL PDANS (0.2 mg mL−1) were added to get the final volume of 400 μL and the fluorescence spectra were recorded after 2 min.

For PKA inhibitor (H-89) detection, the same processes were carried out as above, except that PKA was firstly pretreated with various H-89 concentrations for 1 h. The inhibition efficiency (IE) was determined by the equation: IE (%) = 100 × (I - Ii)/(I - I0), where I0 was the fluorescence intensity of apt-CuNCs/ATP/PDANS ensemble, I was the fluorescence intensity with PKA, and Ii was the fluorescence intensity with both PKA and H-89.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of PKA in HepG2 cell lysates
ce:section.ce:para: HepG2 cells (1 × 106 cells) were cultured in Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM)/F-12 (1:1) supplemented with fetal bovine serum (10%), and the cells were incubated at 37 °C under humidified atmosphere containing CO2 (5%) for 3–4 d. The culture medium was replaced by free medium, and for removing the nutrient solution, the cultured cells were centrifuged at 10,000 rpm and then broken with ultrasonic liquid processor for 2 min. The HepG2 cell lysates were centrifuged more one time and ready for the further experiment. After that, three different PKA concentrations were added into the cell lysates for preparing the spiked cell lysate samples. For the measurement of PKA activity in HepG2 cell lysates, the identical experiments were performed as described previously except that equal volume of above-prepared spiked cell lysate samples replacing pure PKA were added into the sensing system.

In this study, copper ions were reduced to copper atoms by ascorbic acid and interacted with the imidazole group of histidine to form the fluorescence CuNCs [33]. The TEM image (Fig. 1 a) revealed the CuNCs were near spherical and well-monodispersed with an average diameter of 2.03 nm. As shown in Fig. 1b, the typical excitation and emission spectra of CuNCs exhibited peaks around 390 nm and 492 nm, respectively, and the CuNCs solution appeared light yellow in visible light and emitted intense blue-green fluorescence under UV light irradiation (insert of Fig. 1b).

PDANS were obtained by the oxidation and self-polymerization of dopamine in an alkaline environment at room temperature [34]. As showing broad absorbance band in the UV-vis spectrum (Fig. 1c), PDANS can act as one perfect energy acceptor for a variety of donors with different fluorescence emission wavelengths. The FT-IR spectra of dopamine and PDANS were shown in Fig. 1d. As displayed in Fig. 1d, after the self-polymerization of monomer dopamine, the characteristic peaks of dopamine between 500 and 1700 cm−1 nearly disappeared, which was consistent with the previous literature and proved the successful PDANS synthesis [32,35]. In addition, the size of PDANS can be controlled via tuning the polymerization time, and the fluorescence quenching ability of PDANS might be related to their diameters [36], thus three different sizes of PDANS with average diameters of 198, 264 and 332 nm, respectively were synthesized by controlling the polymerization time of 36, 48 and 72 h, respectively (Fig. S1a-c). Fig. S1d showed that PDANS with an average diameter of 264 nm had the best fluorescence quenching ability than the others.

The apt-CuNCs were obtained by the EDC/NHS condensation reaction. Fig. S2a revealed apt-CuNCs retained the similar fluorescence properties of CuNCs except for slight fluorescence intensity enhancement. As displayed in Fig. S2b, compared with CuNCs, a significant new absorption peak at around 260 nm appeared in the absorption spectrum of apt-CuNCs, demonstrating the successful conjugation between aptamer and CuNCs [37,38]. In addition, as shown in Fig. S2c, the zeta potential of CuNCs was −20.1 mV, and after the attachment of aptamer onto CuNCs, the zeta potential of apt-CuNCs was −25.9 mV, which could be due to the negative charge of aptamer and further proved the successful surface functionalization of CuNCs with aptamer [36,39].

The principle of the FRET-based aptasensor by applying CuNCs and PDANS nanomaterials for the detection of PKA activity was illustrated in Scheme 1. The apt-CuNCs were obtained through a traditional EDC/NHS condensation reaction between the carboxy group of CuNCs and amino group of ATP aptamer. Fig. S3 displayed the absorption spectrum of PDANS remarkably overlapped the fluorescence emission spectrum of apt-CuNCs, which indicated the feasibility of energy transfer from apt-CuNCs to PDANSs. Fig. S4 shown that zeta potential of PDANS was −39.6 mV, indicating a negative charge on PDANS surface, and CuNCs were also negative charged, so CuNCs can not be absorbed onto PDANS via electrostatic interaction. However, apt-CuNCs can be spontaneously absorbed onto PDANS surface due to the π-π stacking interactions between nucleobases of aptamer and PDANS [40,41], and TEM images in Fig. S5 further clearly verified that apt-CuNCs were adsorb onto PDANS surface. The binding between aptamer and PDANS brought the fluorescence donor apt-CuNCs in close proximity to the acceptor PDANS surface, and therefore the fluorescence of apt-CuNCs was strongly and effectively quenched via FRET from apt-CuNCs to PDANS. In the presence of ATP, ATP combined with aptamer to form ATP-aptamer complex, which had weak binding ability to PDANS [34,42], causing the dissociation of the apt-CuNCs from the surface of PDANS and bringing the fluorescence recovery of the system. However, with the introduction of PKA, ATP would be translated into ADP by PKA, and ADP had no ability to combine with ATP aptamer, so aptamer was released and apt-CuNCs were absorbed onto PDANS surface again, resulting in the fluorescence decreasing of the system again. Hence PKA activity could be conveniently detected depending on the variation of fluorescence signal.

As shown in Fig. 2 , the fluorescence intensity of apt-CuNCs decreased to 19.6% of the original intensity in the presence of 32 μg mL−1 PDANS while PKA, ATP or PKA/ATP had almost negligible influences on the fluorescence of apt-CuNCs. And 72% fluorescence of CuNCs still retained after it incubated with PDANS under the same conditions due to the lacking ability of unmodified CuNCs to be absorbed onto the surface of PDANS (Fig. S6). Besides, fluorescence increasing of apt-CuNCs/PDANS system was observed when apt-CuNCs were treated with ATP before the addition of PDANS, demonstrating that the fluorescence increasing was due to the combine of ATP with aptamer disturbing the affinity between aptamer and PDANS, which caused apt-CuNCs far away from PDANS surface. The fluorescence intensity of apt-CuNCs/ATP/PDANS system decreased after the addition of PKA due to the translation of ATP into ADP. All the obtained results proved the feasibility of the aptasensor for the detection of PKA activity.

The concentrations of PDANS and ATP, reaction time between apt-CuNCs and PDANS, incubation time between ATP and apt-CuNCs, enzymatic incubation temperature for apt-CuNCs/PKA/ATP/PDANS system were investigated to obtain the optimal performance for PKA activity analysis. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the fluorescence intensity of apt-CuNCs/PDANS system obviously decreased with the increasing concentration of PDANS and reached a plateau when the concentration of PDANS was 32 μg mL−1. Therefore, 32 μg mL−1 PDANS were used in the further experiments. As displayed in Fig. 3b, the fluorescence intensity of apt-CuNCs/ATP/PDANS system gradually increased along with the increasing ATP concentration, and reached the maximum when ATP concentration was 100 μM. Therefore, 100 μM ATP was chosen in the following experiments. Fig. 3c showed that the fluorescence intensity of apt-CuNCs/PDANS system rapidly decreased to the minimum in 2 min and remained stable with increasing reaction time, indicating that aptamer of apt-CuNCs was rapidly absorbed onto PDANS surface and PDANS quenched the fluorescence of apt-CuNCs quickly. Thus, we selected 2 min as the optimal reaction time between apt-CuNCs and PDANS. Fig. 3d revealed that the fluorescence intensity of apt-CuNCs/ATP/PDANS system gradually increased and reached the maximum within 20 min and then remained stable with more time. Therefore, 20 min was chosen for the incubation time between ATP and aptamer of apt-CuNCs. Fig. S7 displayed that the fluorescence intensity of apt-CuNCs/PKA/ATP/PDANS system reached a minimum when the temperature was 37 °C. Therefore, 37 °C was selected as the optimum enzymatic incubation temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of the PKA activity and its inhibitor
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimized conditions, the quantity analysis of PKA activity was performed. The fluorescence emission spectra of apt-CuNCs/ATP/PDANS system with different concentrations of PKA treatment were recorded and displayed in Fig. 4 , it can be seen that the fluorescence intensity gradually decreased with the increasing concentration of PKA. Fig. 4 insert displayed a good linearity between the fluorescence intensity ratio I/I0 (I0 and I were the fluorescence intensity of apt-CuNCs/ATP/PDANS system in the absence and presence of PKA, respectively) and the PKA concentration in the range of 0.05–4.5 U mL−1. The regression equation was I/I0 = 0.992–0.0984CPKA, U mL−1, with corresponding regression coefficient R2 = 0.994, and the detection limit (LOD = 3σ/k, where σ was the standard deviation of the blank signals (n = 11) of apt-CuNCs/ATP/PDANS system and k was the slope of the fluorescence intensity versus PKA concentration) for PKA activity was 0.021 U mL−1. Additionally, in comparation with other reported methods for PKA activity detection summarized in Table S1, this aptasensor provides a comparable quantification range and LOD for PKA activity detection.

Various ions were added into the apt-CuNCs/PKA/ATP/PDANS system to investigate the interference from commonly existing ions in cell lysates for the detection of PKA. As shown in Table S2, the relative error in the presence of the coexisting common cations (Na+, K+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Co2+, Al3+) and anions (Cl−, F−, I−, HCO3 −, NO3 −) was less than 4.2%, indicating that the sensor was largely free from the effect of common ions in the cell lysates. The specificity of the aptasensor with the treatment of different enzymes was further evaluated. As displayed in Fig. 5a, 9 U mL−1 trypsin, urease, glucose oxidase (GOx), tyrosinase and horseradish peroxidase (HRP) both had negligible effect on the fluorescence intensity of apt-CuNCs/ATP/PDANS system in comparison with the treatment of 4.5 U mL−1 PKA. The results demonstrated that this method has good selectivity to PKA over other enzymes.

PKA is one vital target for the clinical treatment of many diseases, thus the screening of effective PKA inhibitor is of great significance in the development of kinase-targeted medicine [43]. In this work, H-89 was chosen as one model of the PKA inhibitor to further test the potential application of this method in inhibitors screening [13]. The fixed concentration of PKA (4.5 U mL−1) was firstly incubated with various concentrations of H-89, and then the detection of the H-89 treated PKA activity was studied by using the method described above. As displayed in Fig. 5 b, the inhibition efficiency (IE) of H-89 increased with the increase of H-89 concentration and the IC50 value (the inhibitor concentration required for half inhibition of the enzyme activity) for H-89 was determined to be 0.17 μM. The results indicated that the aptasensor showed adequate capacity for monitoring kinase inhibitors in a convenient and sensitive way.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PKA determination in HepG2 cell lysates
ce:section.ce:para: In order to further investigate the practical application of the fluorescence aptasensor, the detection of PKA activity in HepG2 cell lysates was carried out. The PKA activity was detected by standard addition method and the obtained results were listed in Table 1 , it can be seen that the relative standard deviation (RSD) was less than 3.2% and average recoveries of PKA activity were in the range of 97.1–104.0%. The above results indicated that the aptasensor can be applied for the detection of PKA activity in real samples and possessed satisfactory precision and accuracy.

In summary, CuNCs (energy donor) and PDANS (energy receptor) were applied to construct one convenient FRET-based aptasensor for PKA activity detection and its inhibitor screening. By taking the virtues of the strong interactions between PDANS and aptamer, specific aptamer-target recognition between ATP and aptamer and excellent quenching ability of PDANS, this aptasensor offered one sensitive and selective way to monitor PKA activity with the detection limit of 0.021 U mL−1. In addition, the aptasensor has been employed for PKA inhibitor screening via choosing H-89 as one model and conducted for detecting PKA activity in HepG2 cell lysates with satisfactory performance, which shows great potential for providing one promising tool for kinase-related biochemical fundamental researches and clinical applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21775052 and No. 21575048), the Science and Technology Development project of Jilin province, China (No. 20180414013GH).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the supplementary data related to this article:
Supplementary data Supplementary data

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2018.06.043.
